Cliffs of Moher rise seven hundred vertical feet from the Atlantic, where winter swells detonate against black shale in eruptions of white spray.
Étretat's chalk arches frame the English Channel, waves threading through L'Aiguille needle rock as centuries of erosion continue their sculptural work.
Big Sur's McWay Falls drops eighty feet directly onto purple sand as Pacific breakers curl around the kelp-wrapped rocks below.
Iceland's Dyrhólaey promontory extends into the North Atlantic, basalt columns receiving the full force of waves that traveled a thousand miles unobstructed.
Twelve Apostles limestone stacks stand isolated off Port Campbell, waves undermining their bases in the relentless siege that will eventually topple them.
Faroe Islands' Vestmanna cliffs rise two thousand feet sheer from the sea, puffin colonies clinging to vertical faces glazed with salt spray.
Patagonia's Marble Caves glow electric blue as glacial meltwater surges through chambers carved over six thousand years in calcium carbonate.
Japan's Tojinbo cliffs present hexagonal basalt columns to the Sea of Japan, geometric formations receiving typhoon-driven waves each autumn.
Oregon's Thor's Well drains seawater into a seemingly bottomless sinkhole, each wave refilling the churning cauldron in endless repetition.
Reynisfjara's black sand beach meets basalt columns as massive sneaker waves surge past sea stacks said to be petrified trolls.
Ireland's Dún Briste sea stack stands isolated fifty meters from the mainland, its flat top crowned with grass unreachable except by helicopter.
California's Bowling Ball Beach reveals hundreds of concretions at low tide, perfectly spherical stones arranged like a giant's game abandoned mid-play.
New Zealand's Pancake Rocks feature limestone layers stacked like dinner plates, blowholes erupting thirty feet high through the gaps during king tides.
Scotland's Old Man of Hoy rises four hundred fifty feet from Orkney waters, climbers challenging the vertical red sandstone as fulmars wheel overhead.
Cornwall's Kynance Cove hides turquoise tide pools among serpentine rock formations, accessible only two hours either side of low water.
Norway's Preikestolen overlooks Lysefjord from two thousand feet, sheer granite dropping into water so deep cruise ships appear miniature far below.
Portugal's Benagil Cave opens its domed ceiling to sky above, waves surging onto the interior beach through a massive arched entrance.
Australia's Gap and Natural Bridge features waves exploding through a cliff-top blowhole, spray raining down on the viewing platform above.
Alaska's Holgate Glacier calves icebergs directly into Kenai Fjords, chunks the size of houses crashing through seal-populated waters below.
Malta's Azure Window collapsed in 2017, but nearby cliffs still show where limestone arches once framed the Mediterranean's impossible blue.
Newfoundland's Gros Morne fjords slice through ancient continental crust, landlocked arms of the Atlantic terminating in thousand-foot canyon walls.
Chile's Amalia Glacier extends blue ice tongues into the Southern Patagonian Ice Field's fjords, calving fronts constantly reshaped by tidal currents.
Ireland's Skellig Michael rises seven hundred feet as a pyramid of stone, medieval monastery perched where Atlantic storms test every handcut stair.
British Columbia's Siwash Rock stands sentinel in Vancouver's harbor, Salish legend made stone resisting the tide that swirls around its base.
Cabo da Roca marks Europe's westernmost point, Portuguese granite cliffs receiving the Atlantic's first landfall after three thousand miles of open ocean.
Tasmania's Tasman Arch spans a chasm where waves compressed by narrowing walls explode upward through the natural bridge's opening.
Santorini's caldera cliffs drop a thousand feet into the submerged volcano, whitewashed villages perched where pyroclastic flows once cascaded into the Aegean.
Washington's Ruby Beach scatters driftwood logs like matchsticks below forested sea stacks, Pacific fog obscuring where stone becomes cloud.
Galicia's Cathedral Beach reveals flying buttress formations at low tide, stone arches connecting the mainland to temporary islands for precious hours.
Lofoten Islands' Kvalvika Beach nestles between vertical granite walls, a crescent of white sand where Arctic waters meet mountains rising straight from shore.
Vietnam's Ha Long Bay scatters three thousand limestone karsts across emerald water, caves and grottoes piercing islands worn narrow at their bases.
Maine's Acadia coastline fractures pink granite into blocks the size of buildings, spruce trees clinging to crevices just beyond the spray line.
South Africa's Hole in the Wall features a circular opening through coastal dolomite, Xhosa sacred site where waves pulse through like a heartbeat.
Sardinia's Grotta di Nettuno requires four hundred steps down the cliff face, interior lake reflecting stalactites in water connected to the Mediterranean below.
Cinque Terre's villages cling to Italian Riviera cliffs, harbor walls rebuilt after each Libeccio storm drives waves over ancient breakwaters.
Kamchatka's Avacha Bay encloses Pacific waters within volcanic peaks, Tri Brata sea stacks guarding the entrance like stone sentinels.
Ireland's Slieve League towers three times higher than the Cliffs of Moher, sheep grazing clifftop pastures a thousand feet above the Atlantic.
Baja California's La Bufadora blowhole shoots seawater a hundred feet skyward, pressure from underwater cave systems timing eruptions with incoming swells.
Greenland's Ilulissat Icefjord discharges twenty billion tons of ice annually, bergs the size of cities drifting past basalt cliffs into Disko Bay.
Scotland's Fingal's Cave extends two hundred feet into Staffa Island, hexagonal basalt columns creating natural acoustics that amplify every wave's resonance.
Cape of Good Hope sees Indian and Atlantic waters collide in standing waves, currents meeting where the continental shelf drops into the Agulhas Basin.
Croatia's Zlatni Rat shifts its pebble spit with seasonal currents, the golden horn extending then retracting into the Adriatic's crystalline blue.
Northern California's Point Reyes reveals its 1906 earthquake offset, cliffs showing the sixteen-foot lateral jump where the San Andreas fault breaks the surface.
New Zealand's Cathedral Cove requires descent through pohutukawa forest, massive limestone arch framing secluded beach accessible only by foot or kayak.
Prince Edward Island's red sandstone cliffs glow rust-orange against the Gulf of St. Lawrence, iron oxide layers eroding into the distinctive russet sand.
Oman's Bimmah Sinkhole opens forty meters from the coast, seawater percolating through limestone to fill the turquoise pool in the desert cliff.
Antarctica's Ross Ice Shelf presents two hundred-foot walls of blue ice to the Southern Ocean, tabular bergs calving from the floating glacier.
Scotland's Duncansby Stacks rise in a row off Caithness, sandstone towers separated from the mainland by narrow channels of churning North Sea.
Spain's Playa de las Catedrales features wave-cut arches and buttresses only visible at low tide, Atlantic waters reclaiming the beach twice daily.
Ireland's Loop Head lighthouse crowns the peninsula's terminus, beam sweeping across the spot where the Shannon's fresh water finally surrenders to salt.
Shetland's Eshaness cliffs showcase contorted red granite twisted by ancient collision, Atlantic gales driving horizontal rain across tortured stone layers above boiling surf.
Chile's Cueva del Milodón opens seventy feet high in Patagonian limestone, where glacial fjord waters lap against the cave floor once trodden by giant ground sloths.
Cornwall's Bedruthan Steps drop massive slate towers onto the beach, each incoming tide isolating the formations as waves carve deeper separation channels annually.
Baffin Island's Sam Ford Fjord presents four thousand vertical feet of granite, the world's tallest coastal cliff plunging through sea ice into Arctic darkness.
Donegal's Sliabh Liag glows amber at sunset, quartzite veins threading through cliffs that descend nearly two thousand feet into the roiling Atlantic chaos.
Olympic Peninsula's Rialto Beach scatters house-sized boulders below forested headlands, each winter storm rearranging the geology that breakers thunder against continuously.
Azores' Furna do Enxofre releases volcanic gases through coastal vents, sulfurous steam mixing with salt spray where lava flows once entered the ocean.
Tasmania's Cape Raoul displays dolerite organ pipes rising three hundred meters, vertical columns forming perfect hexagons that shearwaters nest between at dusk.
Scotland's Smoo Cave combines river waterfall with sea cave, limestone chamber where freshwater plunges forty feet to meet incoming tide surge twice daily.
Big Island's Kīlauea lava delta extends Hawaii into the Pacific, molten rock hissing into steam explosions as two-thousand-degree flows meet cold saltwater.
Pembrokeshire's Green Bridge of Wales spans turquoise water in Carboniferous limestone, arch narrowing annually as storm waves target its keystone from both sides.
Alaska's Glacier Bay presents sixteen tidewater glaciers, ice cliffs calving house-sized chunks that crash through orca-patrolled waters in explosive white plumes.
Japan's Engetsu Island features a circular hole eroded through its center, full moon visible through the aperture during spring tides at precise sunset alignment.
Denmark's Møn's Klint rises four hundred feet in brilliant chalk, fossil-studded white cliffs shedding layers as Baltic waves undermine the Cretaceous formations constantly.
Norway's Trolltunga cantilevers over Ringedalsvatnet from two thousand feet, horizontal rock tongue seeming to float above fjord waters impossibly far below.
South Australia's Remarkable Rocks balance precariously on Kangaroo Island granite, wave-sculpted boulders glowing orange against Southern Ocean turquoise at dawn.
Ireland's Mizen Head connects by footbridge over a chasm, suspension span swaying as Atlantic swells compress through the gorge separating the lighthouse island.
Galapagos' Punta Suarez explodes with each incoming swell, blowhole shooting spray a hundred feet skyward as marine iguanas bask on the surrounding lava.
Adriatic's Durmitor coast reveals submerged caves in Montenegrin limestone, clear water allowing visibility to where channels penetrate two hundred feet beneath the cliffs.
Newfoundland's Mistaken Point preserves Ediacaran fossils in coastal shale, six-hundred-million-year-old impressions visible between tides at this UNESCO site's wave-cut platform.
Washington's Shi Shi Beach aligns sea stacks in receding perspective, Point of the Arches creating stone gateways that frame Pacific sunsets through multiple apertures.
Cyprus' Petra tou Romiou rises from foam where Aphrodite supposedly emerged, limestone stack standing amid breakers in water that glows supernatural blue.
Iceland's Látrabjarg hosts millions of seabirds on Europe's westernmost cliff, puffins nesting in turf at the edge of fourteen-hundred-foot vertical drops.
California's Devil's Slide reveals highway clinging to crumbling cliff face, engineered tunnels now bypassing the section where Pacific erosion constantly threatens the roadbed.
Scotland's Bow Fiddle Rock arches gracefully into Moray Firth, quartzite formation shaped like a violin bow emerging from waters where bottlenose dolphins hunt.
Patagonia's Beagle Channel narrows between Darwin Range glaciers, coastal mountains dropping into water where Fitzroy and Magellan both sailed seeking Pacific passage.
Brittany's Pointe du Raz extends into Atlantic turbulence, Breton granite promontory marking where Raz de Sein currents create standing waves visible from the lighthouse.
Oregon's Cape Perpetua reveals tide pools in Siletzia terrane basalt, ancient oceanic crust pushed onto land forty million years ago, now hosting anemone gardens.
Cayman's Hell formation presents jagged ironshore limestone, fossil coral reef uplifted and sharpened by dissolution, now taking wave spray just above high tide.
Sardinia's Pan di Zucchero rises four hundred feet offshore, limestone monolith turning golden at sunset while peregrine falcons nest on ledges unreachable by climbers.
Hokkaido's Cape Kamui extends into Sea of Japan, basalt cliffs dropping three hundred feet where Ainu legends warn that women's presence angers the ocean gods.
Antrim's Giant's Causeway steps into the North Atlantic, forty thousand hexagonal basalt columns forming geometric platforms where Irish mythology places Finn MacCool's construction.
Alaska's Chirikof Island reveals wave-cut benches at multiple elevations, uplifted terraces recording earthquake history in geological stair-steps above current storm reach.
Normandy's Étretat Manneporte arch dwarfs its famous neighbor, massive opening through chalk cliffs large enough for fishing boats to pass through at high tide.
Vancouver Island's Botanical Beach exposes tide pools carved into sandstone shelves, low spring tides revealing purple sea stars clustering in circular depressions.
Greece's Navagio Beach lies enclosed by two-hundred-meter cliffs, shipwreck rusting on white sand accessible only by boat through turquoise Ionian waters.
Ireland's Downpatrick Head stands separated from the mainland, flat-topped stack with early Christian ruins visible from cliffs across the fifty-meter gap.
Tierra del Fuego's Cape Horn rises fourteen hundred feet from Drake Passage, southernmost rock where Pacific and Atlantic meet in legendary storm-wracked convergence.
Indonesia's Tanah Lot temple crowns an offshore rock, Balinese shrine becoming island twice daily as Indian Ocean tides flood the walking causeway completely.
Queensland's Indian Head rises sixty meters from Fraser Island beach, colored sand cliffs revealing mineral layers while bull sharks patrol the shore break below.
Malta's Dingli Cliffs drop two hundred fifty meters into Mediterranean blue, terraced limestone providing the island's highest elevation point above relentless wave action.
Scotland's Duntulm Castle ruins teeter on Skye's northern cliffs, medieval stronghold abandoned when nursemaid dropped heir apparent from crumbling ramparts to rocks below.
Baja's Todos Santos Island presents granite walls to Pacific swells, big wave break generating thirty-foot faces that thunder against kelp-wrapped boulders during winter.
Wales' Worm's Head extends tidal causeway to serpentine promontory, Carboniferous limestone accessible three hours either side of low water before Atlantic reclaims the path.
South Island's Pancake Rocks layer Dolomite Point in perfectly horizontal strata, blowholes erupting through gaps when southerly swells compress beneath the formations.
Menorca's Cova d'en Xoroi occupies natural caves in coastal cliff, Mediterranean visible through multiple openings where Bronze Age inhabitants once sheltered from pirates.
Donegal's Murder Hole Beach hides beneath four hundred foot cliffs, isolated sand crescent accessible only by rope descent to where Atlantic breakers curl into the amphitheater.
Cinque Terre's Via dell'Amore clings to vertical limestone, lovers' path connecting villages along cliffs where wave spray reaches the trail during Libeccio storms.
Alaska's Misty Fjords reveals granite walls rising three thousand feet, glacially carved channels where waterfalls plunge directly into saltwater patrolled by humpback whales.
Galicia's Costa da Morte scatters shipwreck debris along granite shores, Coast of Death earning its name where Atlantic fury meets Iberia's western extreme.
Skellig Michael's north face drops seven hundred feet of vertical Devonian sandstone into Atlantic chaos, gannet colonies whitewashing ledges unreachable except by abseil.
Amalfi Coast's Fiordo di Furore reveals a hidden fjord carved through Lattari limestone, emerald water surging beneath an arched bridge spanning the fifty-meter gorge.
Alaska's Kenai Fjords present walls of fractured granite dripping with hemlock forest, tidewater glaciers calving blue ice directly into seal-populated channels below.
Faroe Islands' Trælanípa cliff overhangs seven hundred feet of vertical basalt, Lake Sørvágsvatn appearing to float above the Atlantic in optical illusion from below.
Algarve's Ponta da Piedade threads limestone arches and tunnels through golden cliffs, turquoise water illuminating grottoes accessible only by kayak at low tide.
Lofoten's Nusfjord opens between granite walls dropping thirteen hundred feet, Arctic waters calming inside Norway's oldest fishing village sheltered from North Atlantic violence.
Olympic Peninsula's Cape Alava marks the contiguous United States' westernmost point, Makah territory where Pacific breakers explode against basalt plateaus layered with petroglyphs.
Croatia's Blue Cave glows with submerged entrance light on Biševo Island, morning sun refracting through underwater opening to illuminate the grotto's silvery ceiling.
Dorset's Durdle Door arches across a cove in Portland limestone, Jurassic Coast formation framing the English Channel through its weathered span at high tide.
Scotland's Kilt Rock displays vertical basalt columns above a waterfall, Mealt Falls plunging three hundred feet directly into the Sound of Raasay from Skye's eastern coast.
Big Sur's Partington Cove hides behind a tunnel carved through coastal mountains, trail emerging onto a wooden platform where kelp forests sway in transparent turquoise surge.
Japan's Tatsukushi Coast reveals fantastically eroded granite formations, typhoon waves sculpting stone into shapes that gave each stack its descriptive name over centuries.
New Zealand's Muriwai Beach hosts a gannet colony on two offshore stacks, thousands of seabirds nesting on black sand cliffs where Tasman Sea breakers detonate continuously.
Cornwall's Tintagel Island connects by footbridge across a void, medieval castle ruins straddling the gap where King Arthur legends merge with Atlantic spray and Celtic history.
Patagonia's Punta Tombo extends a gravel spit into the South Atlantic, million penguin colony occupying burrows carved into sedimentary cliffs undercut by constant wave action.
Achill Island's Keem Bay nestles between amethyst cliffs, turquoise crescent protected by five-hundred-foot heights that funnel Atlantic winds into standing waves offshore.
Olympic Coast's Abbey Island rises as a forested monolith, sea tunnel piercing its base where Quinault tradition places supernatural beings controlling tide and storm.
Iceland's Kirkjufjara Beach spreads black basalt pebbles below Reynisdrangar stacks, sneaker waves racing up the shore with force that regularly sweeps tourists into hypothermic water.
Cabo San Lucas presents El Arco straddling land's end, granite arch marking where Pacific meets Sea of Cortez in tidal convergence that sea lions navigate expertly.
Scotland's Clachtoll Beach reveals Lewisian gneiss boulders three billion years old, planet's most ancient rock exposed where Assynt coast meets turquoise Highland water clarity.
California's Montana de Oro shows honeycomb weathering in Pismo Formation sandstone, tafoni erosion creating intricate patterns where salt spray accelerates differential dissolution rates.
Andaman Islands' limestone formations tower above tropical water, caves and undercuts hollowing cliffs where swiftlets nest in darkness behind curtains of incoming monsoon surge.
South Island's Cathedral Cliffs reveal perfect organ-pipe dolerite, columnar jointing creating geometric facades rising two hundred feet where Otago Peninsula meets Pacific breakers.
Ireland's Malin Head crowns the island's northern extreme, Inishowen granite cliffs receiving Arctic storm systems that travel south across five hundred miles of unobstructed ocean.
Channel Islands' Painted Cave extends the longest sea cave in world, kayakers penetrating a quarter-mile into Anacapa Island through passages illuminated by bioluminescent plankton.
Hokkaido's Blue Pond reflects volcanic minerals in electric turquoise, though coastal not oceanic, nearby Shiretoko cliffs drop into Sea of Okhotsk through drift-ice zones.
Norway's Geirangerfjord walls rise five thousand feet from UNESCO waters, Seven Sisters waterfalls plunging directly into the channel ferries navigate between vertical wilderness.
Tasmania's Waterfall Bay shows dolerite columns behind a coastal cascade, freshwater plunging eighty feet onto the beach where Southern Ocean surf meets the falling stream.
Santorini's Red Beach spreads rust-colored volcanic cinders below towering oxidized cliffs, swimmers reaching the isolated shore only by boat or scrambling across unstable scree.
Mendocino Headlands reveal wave-cut platforms in Franciscan mélange, winter king tides flooding across the bluffs where cypress trees lean permanent from decades of coastal wind.
Milos Island's Kleftiko presents white volcanic tuff sculpted into arches, former pirate hideout accessible only by boat through turquoise Aegean water illuminating submerged caves.
Shetland's Muckle Flugga lighthouse crowns Britain's northernmost rock, automated beacon standing where Atlantic and North Sea meet in tide races visible from Unst's final cliffs.
Kauai's Nā Pali Coast drops fluted green cliffs four thousand feet, roadless wilderness accessible only by helicopter, boat, or the muddy Kalalau Trail clinging to ancient valleys.
Scotland's Handa Island stacks host great skua colonies, Torridonian sandstone towers rising from waters where puffins dive through tide races hunting sand eels in summer abundance.
Basque Country's Flysch formations layer horizontal strata along the coast, geological calendar pages revealing sixty million years of Earth history between San Sebastian and Bilbao.
Stewart Island's Mason Bay stretches twelve kilometers of wild sand, New Zealand's southernmost beach where kiwi forage at night and surf thunders in from the Roaring Forties.
Galicia's Praia das Catedrais rivals Spain's other Cathedral Beach, distinct formation featuring pointed arches and Gothic-style buttresses exposed only during the lowest spring tides.
Vancouver Island's Mystic Beach requires rainforest descent to reach, suspension bridge crossing creek before trail emerges where surge channels explode through sandstone shelves constantly.
Sardinia's Cala Goloritzé drops limestone pinnacles to turquoise Mediterranean, accessible only by boat or mountain trail descending to the beach banned from anchorage to protect it.
Ireland's Horn Head towers six hundred feet above Donegal's northern water, coastal drive circumnavigating the promontory where razorbills nest on Atlantic-facing precipices each spring.
Alaska's Chiswell Islands host Steller sea lion rookeries on granite stacks, Kenai Fjords tour boats circling carefully as three-thousand-pound bulls defend territories with resonant roars.
Victoria's Loch Ard Gorge preserves shipwreck history in its sheltered inlet, two teenage survivors reached shore here when clipper struck reef in eighteen seventy-eight.
Cornwall's Lizard Point marks England's southernmost extreme, serpentine rock glowing red-green where Atlantic currents accelerate around the peninsula through treacherous shipping lanes.
Newfoundland's L'Anse aux Meadows shows Viking settlement beside coastal terraces, Norse explorers choosing this beach where salmon rivers meet the Strait of Belle Isle's icy water.
Oregon's Secret Beach hides south of Samuel Boardman corridor, trail descending through Sitka spruce to an isolated cove where driftwood accumulates against offshore rock formations.
Iceland's Garður lighthouses stand paired on Reykjanes Peninsula, old and new beacons marking where pilots navigate between Atlantic swells and the geothermal steam of volcanic vents.
Scotland's St Kilda archipelago rises sheer from Atlantic isolation, Britain's remotest islands abandoned in nineteen thirty when cliff-nesting seabirds no longer sustained human population.
Baja's La Paz malecón curves along Sea of Cortez turquoise, but north the Sierra meets Pacific in granite cliffs that gray whales navigate during their annual migration.
Ireland's Ceide Fields reveal Stone Age walls beneath coastal bog, five-thousand-year-old farming patterns extending to cliff edges where Atlantic erosion gradually reclaims archaeological evidence.
Lord Howe Island's Ball's Pyramid rises eighteen hundred feet from the Tasman Sea, world's tallest sea stack supporting the last population of stick insects thought extinct.
Achill Island's Cathedral Rocks rise as natural flying buttresses of quartzite, Atlantic swells compressing through narrow channels to erupt sixty feet upward in pressurized geysers.
Norway's Hornelen cliff towers almost three thousand feet directly from Hornelensvatnet's fjord waters, Europe's highest sea cliff dwarfing fishing vessels passing through its shadow below.
Cape Kiwanda's sandstone allows wave-carved honeycomb caves at base level, Oregon beachgoers exploring tafoni chambers until incoming tide floods the porous formations completely.
Pembrokeshire's Elegug Stacks stand as twin limestone towers off Flimston coast, peregrine falcons nesting on ledges while grey seals haul out on wave-polished platforms between them.
Sligo's Mullaghmore Head reveals horizontal Dartry limestone beds, Atlantic groundswell wrapping around the peninsula where each autumn storm peels away another geological layer.
Shetland's Ronas Voe cuts deep into red granite hills, glacially carved fjord where otters hunt among kelp forests clinging to walls that plunge a hundred feet underwater.
Alaska's Hubbard Glacier advances six miles wide into Disenchantment Bay, tidewater ice cliff calving icebergs that roll and shatter through harbor seal nursing grounds continuously.
Donegal's Tormore Island connects by tombolo at spring lows, sand bridge appearing twice monthly to let hikers reach the grass-topped stack before tide reclaims the causeway.
Cornwall's Porthcurno Beach glows turquoise between granite headlands, Minack Theatre carved into cliff face providing amphitheater seating where actors perform above crashing Atlantic accompaniment.
Iceland's Hjörleifshöfði promontory rises three hundred feet from black Mýrdalssandur plains, basalt mesa isolated when glacial floods carved channels that now channel North Atlantic breakers.

Galicia's Praia de Augas Santas hides below eucalyptus forest, stone stairway descending to a cove where freshwater spring feeds pools separated from ocean by low tide barrier.
Scotland's Stac Pollaidh overlooks Enard Bay from inland, but coastal Rubha Coigeach reveals Torridonian sandstone sea cliffs glowing rust-red against turquoise summer water clarity.
Northern Ireland's Carrick-a-Rede rope bridge sways sixty feet above channel to salmon fishery island, tourists crossing the span where Atlantic surge compresses through the gap.
Big Sur's Partington Point hides a hand-carved tunnel to smuggler's cove, prohibition-era passage emerging where redwood-shaded creek meets Pacific through a natural rock archway.
Faroe Islands' Múlafossur waterfall drops straight from Lake Leitisvatn into the Atlantic, cascade plunging two hundred feet to ocean spray where puffins nest on surrounding cliffs.
Tasmania's Fossil Bluff exposes Permian mudstone layers along Wynyard coast, two-hundred-seventy-million-year-old shellfish impressions visible between tides in the wave-cut platform.
Japan's Yonaguni Monument shows geometric underwater terraces, controversial formation lying eighty feet down where currents sweep past angular sandstone blocks possibly carved by ancient humans.
Norway's Seven Sisters peaks drop directly into Geirangerfjord from fifty-five-hundred-foot summits, vertical mile descending from snowfield to saltwater through unbroken wilderness.
Oregon's Arch Rock spans Brookings coastline in grey sandstone, natural bridge framing Pacific sunsets while western gulls nest on the formation's grass-topped crown each spring.
Skellig Beag rises spiky and uninhabited beside its monastery neighbor, gannet colony whitewashing Devonian sandstone pinnacles too steep even for medieval monks seeking extreme isolation.
Kamchatka's Russkaya Bay encloses waters within volcanic caldera walls, thermal vents heating tide pools where king crabs grow enormous among coastal fumaroles steaming at surf line.
Wales' Rhossili Bay curves three miles of sand below six-hundred-foot Rhossili Down, Worm's Head promontory extending from the southern terminus accessible only at precise tidal windows.
Sardinia's Capo Caccia drops limestone cliffs into Neptune's Grotto, six hundred fifty steps descending the vertical face to sea level cave entrance only accessible during calm conditions.
South Africa's Robberg Peninsula extends four kilometers into the Indian Ocean, three hiking loops traversing sandstone cliffs where Cape fur seals bark from rocks below the isthmus.
Scotland's Isle of Staffa reveals not just Fingal's Cave but entire island of hexagonal basalt, cooling lava columns creating geometric coastline that inspired Mendelssohn's Hebrides Overture.
Greenland's Uummannaq Island rises four thousand feet as a heart-shaped mountain from Baffin Bay, Inuit town clinging to the base where fishing boats navigate through summer's melting ice.
California's Natural Bridges State Beach shows final remaining arch after two collapsed, Monterey Bay mudstone formation hosting monarch butterfly eucalyptus grove above tide pool terraces.
Croatia's Stiniva Cove opens through a narrow cliff gap to hidden beach, twenty-foot-wide entrance concealing turquoise swimming hole until hikers descend the final steep scramble.
Alaska's Columbia Glacier retreats rapidly into Prince William Sound, tidewater terminus now twelve miles back from nineteen eighty position leaving behind iceberg-choked fjord waters.
Ireland's Great Blasket Island shows abandoned village facing Atlantic violence, islanders evacuated in nineteen fifty-three when winter storms made the passage to mainland too dangerous.
Shetland's St Ninian's Isle connects by Europe's finest tombolo, shell-sand isthmus appearing at mid-tide to link the chapel ruins where Pictish treasure was discovered in buried hoard.
Olympic Peninsula's Point of Arches clusters sea stacks at Shi Shi Beach northern terminus, Makah territory where twenty-seven sandstone formations create natural cathedral of stone.
Brittany's Île Vierge lighthouse rises three hundred feet as world's tallest, granite tower marking where Aber Wrac'h estuary meets Atlantic currents sweeping past Finistère's rocky coast.
Hokkaido's Cape Sōya points toward Sakhalin across La Pérouse Strait, Japan's northernmost point where Sea of Okhotsk meets Sea of Japan in current convergence visible from monument.
Cornwall's Pedn Vounder Beach requires steep descent through private property, secluded cove below Treryn Dinas Iron Age fort where clothing-optional sunbathers avoid main tourist crowds.
Patagonia's Pio XI Glacier extends thirty miles into Eyre Fjord, Southern Ice Field's longest glacier advancing against global trends while calving building-sized bergs into Chilean channels.
Ireland's Mamore Gap descends Inishowen's highest road to coast, switchbacks dropping from mountain pass to Tullagh Bay where Atlantic surf pounds remote Donegal sand and stone.
Scotland's Ardnamurchan Point marks Britain's mainland's westernmost extent, Kilchoan lighthouse crowning volcanic peninsula where Hebridean basalt meets Loch Sunart's sheltered waters.
New Zealand's White Island steams actively eight kilometers offshore, Whakaari volcano's crater walls dropping into Bay of Plenty through sulfurous vents that killed tourists in twenty nineteen.
Alaska's LeConte Glacier calves into fjord bearing its name, southernmost tidewater glacier in Northern Hemisphere creating iceberg gardens where harbor seals give birth on floating platforms.
Menorca's Cala Macarella curves white sand between limestone cliffs, Mediterranean pine forest descending to turquoise bay accessible by coastal trail from Ciutadella through fragrant scrubland.
Scotland's Cape Wrath reaches for its violent name at Britain's northwestern extreme, Clo Mor cliffs rising nine hundred feet where Atlantic storms arrive unimpeded from Arctic latitudes.
California's Pfeiffer Beach glows purple from manganese garnet sand, Big Sur's hidden cove requiring unmarked turnoff to reach the shore where Keyhole Arch frames Pacific through stone.
Iceland's Jökulsárlón glacial lagoon drains through short channel to Diamond Beach, icebergs from Breiðamerkurjökull washing ashore on black sand where they sparkle like scattered gems.
Galicia's Cabo Ortegal marks where granite meets schist in geological boundary, Hercynian cliffs dropping into Atlantic swells that arrive from four thousand miles of unobstructed fetch.
Shetland's Out Stack rises as Britain's northernmost point, skerry beyond Muckle Flugga where gannet colonies occupy the rock that marks transition from North Sea to Norwegian waters.
Oregon's Face Rock rises as Coos County landmark, Bandon Beach legend telling of frozen Indian maiden visible in sandstone profile above surf where offshore monoliths cluster.
Norway's Akrafjorden penetrates Hordaland mountains beneath Folgefonna glacier, fjord walls rising six thousand feet from water so deep that cruise ships navigate channels barely wider than vessels.

Vancouver Island's Botanical Beach State Park exposes shale and sandstone tide pools, Port Renfrew's scientific reserve where purple ochre stars cluster in circular depressions carved by grinding pebbles.
